item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
All images are under Public Domain.

All images are compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0 and tags have been updated to reflect this.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Aww dang, I could have sworn those were both CC when I found them, but maybe the Me from 2014 was an idiot.

I found my original source for oldman.jpg here, but I don't think it's still covered.

I also found the source for oldwoman.jpg in this article, but my gut tells me that won't be covered either.

Oh well. If I find some suitable replacements that are compliant, I'll edit them in. I don't think it'll hurt the article too much without them, but I liked having them there to break up the walls of text.

EDIT: I've replaced the images with some CC-compliant ones from Wikimedia Commons. The exact sources are linked in the images' Info.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Neither oldwoman.jpg or oldman.jpg could be sourced reliably and have since been removed under the assumption that they are not CC 3.0 compliant.

item.title: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Note: Image sources of "oldwoman.jpg" and "oldman.jpg" are still unknown.

  • valley.jpg

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public domain

Title: File:Elk Complex Fire Boise National Forest.jpg
Author: U.S. Department of Agriculture
Uploaded on: 2014

item.title: 200th upvote!
item.content:encoded:
I’m glad my +1 gave this article a milestone! And boy, was this +1 earned. I really liked how it was described that…
+ show block
– hide block


the storyteller was “drinking” Mabel’s story, or perhaps her hatred? Either way, this family feud kinda thing quickly turned into its own, tragic story. By to mention the neat Southern Americana kinda aesthetic to the setting. Really sells me on the biblical stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally assumed that the gloves were used to suffocate people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to translate 2039 into Russian, but I can't understand: is Dr. Wilkes male or female? There is the declensional difference in Russian.

Is he bothering you?

No further questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: mein good men. this is epic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So that in the nature of man, we find three principal causes of quarrel. First, competition; secondly, fear; thirdly, glory.

The first maketh men invade for gain; the second, for safety; and the third, for reputation.

The first use violence, to make themselves masters of other men’s persons, wives, children, and cattle; the second, to defend them; the third, for trifles, as a word, a smile, a different opinion, and any other sign of undervalue, either direct in their persons or by reflection in their kindred, their friends, their nation, their profession, or their name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think reanimation would imply that the dead bodies rise up, rather than them all waking up in their homes afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty great. There are some issues, which have been mentioned by others. I'm giving an upvote though, simply because I like this concept, and the article overall, to much to see it go.

The entry about the Super Soakers gave me a good chuckle. The arrow to the knee part did make me want to hunt you down and smack you in the head, but I saw you said that it wasn't intentional and I'm choosing to believe you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I was a bit nervous about the expungement on the gloves, but I'm happy with the unsettling tone it gave off. Like, how the shit do you murder people using thick woolen gloves? I definitely have my own idea about what they're used for, but I thought it would add a bit of creep factor to leave that little tidbit to the imagination

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's still a bit too long, but I think it's at the point where it pays off enough to work for me. +1

It's the anomalous weapons (or "weapons") that really make this. Immortal Hatfields & McCoys isn't interesting enough — but that list is pretty entertaining. It's not just the nuclear weapons, it's the nuclear weapons and the super soakers that are literally just fucking super soakers (and the implication that sometimes they have to re-figure-out that the super soakers are only shooting water).

I'll also note that you have an unusual case of old-style expungement used well, with the thick woolen gloves. Normally, that kind of expungement doesn't work — you just fill in "Subsequent testing of water reveals that it is 2SPOOKY4ME". But in this case, the rest of the list is already fairly oddball, and the gloves by themselves aren't already a 2spooky element, and the act of throwing them into the river (why the river? we wonder), and the dissolving, and their very inclusion on the list (why thick woolen gloves?), is all very strange. Then you're left wondering what the fuck could be expunged there? and it turns out to be actually sorta creepy after all, or at least intriguing.

item.title: Updates
item.content:encoded:
Took advice from the folks who said the containment procedures were too long. Whittled them down a bit; the important bits are still there, just took out some unnecessary fluff.

Also took advice from a few people who said that it takes too long to get to the actual anomalous stuff. I took the last paragraph of the description, tweaked it a bit, and moved it much closer to the beginning. I think the main part of the article is easier to get into now, as you're not wading through a bunch of non-anomalous detail to get to the weird stuff.

…Also removed a few useless year redactions, and added in an item in the object log that actually refers to the article's main picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you think the interview would flow better if it wasn't so "wall-of-text"? Or would it benefit more from just cutting some stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it quite a bit. I wasn't as thrilled with the version I read in Drafts and Critiques, but I guess that was updated somewhat. It's an obvious reference to the phenomenon of literal family feuds, but I've never seen an SCP quite like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this. I thought the last interview dragged on a bit, but I liked the weapons and the effect, and the fact that it went a little more beyond what I expected it to do. S'pretty cool. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

I agree with you in that the article could stand to be shortened a bit. I've already started chipping away at it (including the redactions).

In terms of the awkward dialogue, could you give me a couple of examples?

Regarding the bible verses, that wasn't necessarily for the sake of the anomaly itself, it was more just to illustrate the extreme distress Mabel felt when she was reminded of her ultimate fate. My idea of an emotional breakdown for an old lady in the Bible belt was freaking out and reciting scripture.

+ Spoiler-ish
- hide block

The first verse (Matt 7:20) refers to an arboreal metaphor that Jesus used for false prophets (the storyteller), while the second (2 Thess 1:9) refers to being eternally shut out from the glory of God (she can never die). Both of these ideas are on her mind at the time, hence the scriptural logorrhoea.


In my mind, they're not necessarily out of context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty neutral here. It just doesn't really grab me.
Things I like: The concept, and the execution.
Things I don't like: Wordy containment procedures, some unnecassary redactions in the weapons chart, occasionally awkward dialogue, and some random out of context Bible verses that just seem for the heck of it.
No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it was intentional; but I just did it for a chuckle, really. Glad someone noticed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quick question:

Potential spoiler?
saxophone horse strikes again

Are the names "Blaine and Mabel" supposed to sound like Cain and Abel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few questionable redactions, and a few tonal errors. But overall, though it may be a cliche, it's a well-written and quite compelling cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty damn great for a first try. I agree with some of what Kalinin said above, but I enjoyed the story enough to give you an upvote.

Good first try rubik!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Understood. So is the "Tag Guide" just out of date then? I'm sure more newbies like me would appreciate it if the Tag Guide just said exactly what you told me. Just a newb's two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please don't do your own tagging in the future. We have a staff team that takes care of this, and doing it yourself actually makes it more difficult for us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. I did get a little caught up in the technicalities. I've already started chipping away to make it a bit shorter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After Lucy, she pretty much lost hope for happiness, so she just settled down with Walter Pike. The Pikes just so happened to live across the valley from where Blaine settled his family. Are there any particular examples of tone you could point out that I could work on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this, but I do strongly agree that it needs some editing for length: The payoff is good, but it takes much too long to actually get there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty nice for a first attempt… But why did Mabel never try to move away from Blaine? You'd think she would've at least considered it. Tentative upvote, as the concept and story is nice, but the tone is lacking in spots. I do like the dialogue; it feels pretty genuine.
EDIT: Actually, the tone is pretty solid, and I have no idea what I was talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also agree that "slaughter and resurrection" is a bit non-clinical

Maybe "death and reanimation"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, honestly.

I agree with your opinion of the unnecessary black box on the date. Does the same apply to the dates in the object log? Or are the years sufficient for those?

I also agree that "slaughter and resurrection" is a bit non-clinical, but I racked my brain for another way to phrase that. (Cue over 9000 responses containing perfectly acceptable examples that I could not come up with)

I took an arrow in the knee

ಠ_ಠ

I really did not even notice that until you pointed it out. That's just the way my grandmother would've said it. Never put two and two together, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not a bad attempt. Part of me does think, however, that this was really just a giant excuse to have hillbillies firing nuclear weapons at each other (which is admittedly sort of funny).

I have some issues with your execution. This has plainly been painstakingly developed in terms of capturing scientific tone, which is generally good for writing on this site. However, I feel that's it been taken a little far here, and what you have is excessively detailed and lengthy descriptions that are taking away from the flow of the article. This shows up in the long, dragging feel of the containment procedures, which I felt to be a little overly specific. You've also buried the lede considerably here; I was most of the way through the article before it became apparent to me why this was an anomalous phenomenon and not a real-world family feud.

Conceptually, I feel there's something there. Even though it's been done before, "recurring loop of the damned" is a tried-and-true storytelling device, and there's some potential there. Like others, though, I feel like this could have been taken into more strange and unusual places than "Hatfields and McCoys shoot laser beams at each other forever". The stranger in the old woman's story is a little too "stock devil at the crossroads" for my tastes to really serve to take the story in that direction. Also, I found it really odd that the old lady hadn't bothered to consider the true ramifications of what was going on until she was interviewed by the researcher. Like, it just then occurred to her that this was a "for-ev-er" deal? Seemed odd.

Lastly, some nitpicks:

Since the Foundation's discovery of SCP-2039 in 190█,

This makes no sense to me. What possible situation could come up where it's okay for the reader to know that the Foundation discovered this between 1901-1909, but not the specific year? Seems like it would be easier to black out the entire year, or say "the early 20th century", or something.

Researchers theorize that, until a solution is found, SCP-2039 will continue the cycle of slaughter and resurrection indefinitely.

"Cycle of slaughter and resurrection" feels overly dramatic for a clinical article. There's probably a more scientific way to describe what you're referring to there.

I took an arrow in the knee,

ಠ_ಠ

Too many issues for me to support this, but a promising start, I'd say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

Though I felt like I was really being pushy every time I came into chat.

dierubikdie entered #site19

dierubikdie: heys guys whatchu talkin' bout that's nice read my draft k thanx bye

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know of the Hatfields and McCoys but never actually bothered to learn the story of it.

For a n00b's first time effort, this sure made my own first SCP look like a pile of crap by comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not doing it for me, just because, as mentioned before, it's basically the Hatfield-McCoy feud, skipified, taken to eleven, and largely played straight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to the fine folks over in chat for their wonderful feedback and suggestions. My first mainlist article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I literally just put this up. Give me twelve seconds to upload the pictures.

EDIT: That's kind of the point. A family feud, taken way too far by the originators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is cliche, and the pictures haven't been properly formatted.

EDIT: Fair enough, the pictures have been fixed. This concept is still just a take on the Hatfield and McCoy story, though.
